main_summary.summary: Rev. Charles Sherrod, SNCC field secretary, was interviewed by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill on 4 June 2011 in Albany, Georgia, during the Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement 53rd Reunion. Sherrod recounts arriving in Albany around October 1961 with prior experience from Rock Hill, South Carolina, where he had served on a chain gang, and describes how the eighth chapter of Romans sustained him in the work that followed. He explains how he, Charles A. Jones, and Cordell Reagon obtained permission from Albany's adult leadership, including the Criterion Club, to bring SNCC organizers into the city, then built youth participation through small church meetings and nonviolent direct-action workshops. He details the bus-station integration test, the first mass march of 255 people that filled Shiloh Baptist Church and Mount Zion Baptist Church, Laurie Pritchett's strategy of dispersing arrestees across Americus, Terrell County, Baker County, and Mitchell County jails, and the picketing campaign against segregated downtown stores. Sherrod also describes the Baker County voter-registration march at which he was beaten with ax handles and saved by an elder workshop participant, and closes by noting the reunion-week announcements of a radio-station license and a New Communities Inc. land contract.
key_themes: Faith and biblical grounding for movement work
main_summary.historical_significance:
Sherrod's first-person account documents the founding architecture of the Albany Movement from the perspective of its lead SNCC field secretary, including the consent-from-elders protocol used to enter Albany, the recruitment of four neighborhood gangs into the nonviolent campaign, and the Criterion Club's prior civic-petition work that preceded SNCC's arrival. His testimony names the specific churches that opened first (the Presbyterian church in the city) and that held the first mass meeting (Shiloh and Mount Zion Baptist), and places Laurie Pritchett's multi-county-jail dispersal tactic within the lived organizing response that turned arrest-sites into new organizing sites across Americus, Terrell, Baker, and Mitchell counties.

The interview also preserves Sherrod's eyewitness account of the Baker County voter-registration march beating, including the role of an elder participant trained in his nonviolent direct-action workshops who shielded him with her body and ended the assault. Recorded at the Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement 53rd Reunion, it captures Sherrod's framing of the radio-station license and New Communities Inc. land contract as the contemporary continuation of the 1961-62 work.

chapters.title: Arrival in Albany and Romans 8
chapters.summary: Sherrod describes arriving in Albany, Georgia around October 1961 with the chain-gang experience from Rock Hill, South Carolina behind him. He explains how the eighth chapter of Romans grounded his conviction that nothing but death could stop the mission of developing his people.

chapters.title: Reading the Community's Fear
chapters.summary: Sherrod recounts learning how to reach the elder generation who would not lift their eyes to whites, the indignity of Black women required to wear stocking caps before trying on hats in segregated stores, and the fear of confronting the county sheriff. He describes the prior civic work of the Criterion Club petitioning the City Commission for paved streets and lighting.

chapters.title: Building the Youth Base
chapters.summary: Sherrod describes obtaining permission from Albany's adults alongside Charles A. Jones and Cordell Reagon, recruiting young people first into small church meetings (the Presbyterian church opened first), and bringing in the east, west, north, and south side neighborhood gangs as nonviolent marchers. He emphasizes the weekly nonviolence preparation and the rule against switchblade knives in the church before marches.

chapters.title: First Marches and Jail Strategy
chapters.summary: Sherrod recounts the bus-station integration test with Jones and Cordell Reagon, the first mass march of 255 people that filled Shiloh Baptist Church and Mount Zion Baptist Church, and Laurie Pritchett's strategy of distributing arrestees to jails in Americus, Terrell County, Baker County, and Mitchell County, Georgia. He notes the picketing campaign against downtown stores and the Smith Grocery closure after two days.

chapters.title: Baker County Beating and the Reunion
chapters.summary: Sherrod describes the Baker County, Georgia voter-registration march of approximately 150 people from the church to the courthouse, the white men with ax handles who beat him to the ground, and the elder workshop participant whose shielding action ended the assault. He closes by noting the Southwest Georgia 53rd Reunion week's signing of a radio-station license and a New Communities Inc. land contract.
related_events: Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement 53rd Reunion 2011
